questions.to_find: The output printed by the given C program that reverses characters in a char array using a for loop

understand.plain: The program initializes a 6-element char array with 'world' (characters 'w','o','r','l','d','\0' at indices 0-5). The for loop tries to reverse the string in-place by copying a[j] into a[i] while moving i forward and j backward. However, the first assignment copies the null terminator ('\0') into a[0], making the string appear empty when printed.

keywords.explain: The special character '\0' (ASCII 0) that marks the end of a C string. printf stops printing when it encounters this character.
keywords.example: "hello" is stored as {'h','e','l','l','o','\0'} — printf stops at '\0'.

keywords.explain: A technique that swaps elements from both ends of an array toward the center to reverse it, without using extra storage.
keywords.example: Swapping a[0] with a[4], then a[1] with a[3] reverses a 5-element array.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='520' height='200' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><text x='20' y='28' class='lbl ink'>Initial: a = "world"</text><rect x='20' y='40' width='60' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='50' y='72' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>w</text><text x='50' y='105' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>0</text><rect x='90' y='40' width='60' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='120' y='72' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>o</text><text x='120' y='105' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>1</text><rect x='160' y='40' width='60' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='190' y='72' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>r</text><text x='190' y='105' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><rect x='230' y='40' width='60' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='260' y='72' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>l</text><text x='260' y='105' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><rect x='300' y='40' width='60' height='50' class='bg'/><text x='330' y='72' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>d</text><text x='330' y='105' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>4</text><rect x='370' y='40' width='60' height='50' class='hi'/><text x='400' y='72' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>\0</text><text x='400' y='105' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>5</text><text x='20' y='140' class='tag ink'>Step 1 (i=0,j=5): a[0] = a[5] = '\0'</text><text x='20' y='165' class='tag no'>a[0] becomes '\0' → printf stops immediately → Null string</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array 'world\0' with indices 0-5. The loop first copies a[5]='\0' into a[0], making the string null-terminated at position 0, so printf prints nothing (null string).

given.aim: Determine what printf prints after the for loop modifies the char array

terms.meaning: Character array of size 6 initialized to {'w','o','r','l','d','\0'} at indices 0 through 5
terms.connects: The null terminator at index 5 is the key element that gets copied to index 0

terms.meaning: Loop with update expression a[i++]=a[j--]: copies a[j] into a[i], then increments i and decrements j
terms.example: First execution of update: a[0]=a[5]='\0', then i becomes 1, j becomes 4
terms.connects: The update runs BEFORE the next condition check — first copy happens when i=0, j=5

terms.meaning: Prints the string starting at a[0] until a null terminator '\0' is found
terms.example: If a[0]='\0', printf prints nothing (empty/null string)
terms.connects: Since a[0]='\0' after the first loop update, printf prints a null/empty string

given.plan: Trace the for loop iteration by iteration. In a for loop, the update expression (a[i++]=a[j--]) executes at the END of each iteration (after the body, which is empty here). Actually for a for loop, the update runs after each iteration. The first check is i=0,j=5: 0<5 is true, body is empty (;), then update a[0]=a[5]='\0', i becomes 1, j becomes 4. After a[0]='\0', when printf prints a, it immediately hits '\0' at index 0 and prints nothing.

steps.title: Trace the for loop execution
steps.apply:
Initial state: i=0, j=5, a={'w','o','r','l','d','\0'}.
Iteration 1: check i<j → 0<5 true; body is empty (;); update: a[0]=a[5]='\0', i++ makes i=1, j-- makes j=4.
Iteration 2: check i<j → 1<4 true; body empty; update: a[1]=a[4]='d', i=2, j=3.
Iteration 3: check i<j → 2<3 true; body empty; update: a[2]=a[3]='l', i=3, j=2.
Iteration 4: check i<j → 3<2 false; loop ends.
Final array: {'\0','d','l','l','d','\0'}.
steps.note: The critical observation: a[5]='\0' (null terminator) is copied into a[0] in the very first update step, before any visible character is written.

steps.title: Determine printf output
steps.apply: After the loop, a[0]='\0'. printf("%s\n", a) starts at a[0], immediately encounters '\0', and prints nothing (an empty/null string followed by a newline).
steps.note: The answer is B. Null string — the string is effectively empty because its first character is the null terminator.

solution.result: B. Null string — printf prints an empty string because a[0] becomes '\0' after the first loop update.

questions.formula_note: C string null-terminator rule: printf %s stops at the first '\0'. In a for loop, the update expression executes after the (empty) body each iteration, so the first update copies a[5]='\0' into a[0].

questions.to_find: The values of i and a[i] printed by the C program after two loops modify the array

understand.plain: The program has an array {1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8}. The first for loop increments certain elements by 1 (with an extra i++ inside that makes it skip indices). After the loop, i is decremented. The second for loop uses a local variable also named i (which shadows the outer i) and decrements certain array elements. The final printf uses the outer i and a[outer i].

keywords.explain: When a local variable declared inside a block has the same name as an outer variable, the inner declaration hides (shadows) the outer one within that block.
keywords.example: In the second for loop body, 'int i = j/2' creates a new local i that is separate from the outer i declared in main.

keywords.explain: The first loop increments i both in the loop update (i++) and inside the body (i++), so i advances by 2 each iteration, causing the loop to visit only even indices.
keywords.example: Starting at i=0: after body i=1, after update i=2; next body i=3, after update i=4; now i=4 >= 3, loop ends.

i=3</text><text x='10' y='135' class='tag ink'>Loop2 (j=7,6,5): a[3]-=1, a[3]-=1, a[2]-=1 → a[3]=2, a[2]=3</text><rect x='130' y='145' width='55' height='45' class='no'/><text x='157' y='172' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>3</text><text x='157' y='200' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>[2]</text><rect x='190' y='145' width='55' height='45' class='no'/><text x='217' y='172' class='lbl ink' text-anchor='middle'>2</text><text x='217' y='200' class='sub mut' text-anchor='middle'>[3]</text><text x='270' y='172' class='tag ink'>i=3, a[3]=2 → prints 3, 2</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Array modification trace: first loop increments a[0] and a[2] by 1 (visiting indices 0 and 2, skipping by 2 each time). After i-- outer i becomes 3. Second loop with j from 7 to 5 decrements a[3] twice and a[2] once. Final: i=3, a[3]=2, output is '3, 2'.

given.aim: Trace both for loops and the i-- statement to find the values printed by printf

terms.term: First for loop with double i++
terms.meaning: Loop runs with i++ in both the body and the for-update, causing i to increment by 2 per iteration, so only even indices are processed
terms.example: i=0: a[0]+=1, body i++=1, update i++=2; i=2: a[2]+=1, body i++=3, update i++=4; i=4>=3 stop
terms.connects: After the loop i=4; only a[0] and a[2] were incremented

terms.meaning: Decrements the outer i from 4 to 3 before the second loop
terms.connects: The outer i=3 is what gets printed by printf at the end

terms.term: Second loop with local int i = j/2
terms.meaning: Declares a NEW local variable i inside the loop body, shadowing the outer i; this local i is used to index the array
terms.connects: The outer i remains 3 throughout the second loop; only a[2] and a[3] are decremented

given.plan: Trace the first loop to find which array elements change and the final value of outer i. Apply i--. Trace the second loop noting that 'int i = j/2' is a local shadow variable, so the outer i stays at 3. Compute a[3] after both loops and read the printf output.

steps.title: Trace the first for loop
steps.apply:
Initial array: a={1,2,3,4,5,6,7,8}.
Iteration 1: i=0, 0<3 true; a[0]=1+1=2; body i++ → i=1; loop update i++ → i=2.
Iteration 2: i=2, 2<3 true; a[2]=3+1=4; body i++ → i=3; loop update i++ → i=4.
Iteration 3: i=4, 4<3 false; loop ends.
Array after loop 1: a={2,2,4,4,5,6,7,8}. Outer i=4.
steps.note: Only indices 0 and 2 were modified. The double increment (body i++ and loop i++) means the effective step is 2.

steps.title: Apply i-- and trace the second for loop
steps.note: The 'int i = j/2' inside the second loop body is a completely separate local variable — it does NOT modify the outer i=3. This is the key insight that makes the answer C (3,2) rather than D (3,3).

solution.result: C. 3,2 — the outer i=3 and a[3]=2 after both loops execute.

questions.formula_note: Variable shadowing in C: a local declaration 'int i = ...' inside a block creates a new variable that hides (shadows) the outer i for that block's scope only; the outer i is unaffected. Also: integer division j/2 truncates toward zero.
